chapters.notes: Euclid's Division Lemma: For any positive integers a and b, there exist unique integers q and r such that a = bq + r, 0 ≤ r < b. Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic: Every composite number can be expressed (factorised) as a product of primes, and this factorisation is unique, apart from the order. HCF (a, b) × LCM (a, b) = a × b. For rational numbers p/q in simplest form, decimal expansion terminates if denominator is of the form 2^m × 5^n; else it is non-terminating repeating. Irrational numbers: Numbers that cannot be expressed in the form p/q. √2, √3, √5, π etc. are irrational. Proofs often use contradiction assuming a rational form.

questions.q: For some integer m, every even integer is of the form:
questions.exp: An even integer is divisible by 2. By Euclid's division lemma, any integer can be written as 2m or 2m+1. For even integers, remainder is 0, so form is 2m.

questions.q: If HCF(26, 169) = 13, then LCM(26, 169) is:
questions.exp: Using HCF × LCM = product of numbers: 13 × LCM = 26 × 169 => LCM = (26 × 169)/13 = 2 × 169 = 338.

questions.q: Which of the following is an irrational number?
questions.exp: √(4/9) = 2/3 (rational); √12/√3 = √4 = 2 (rational); √81 = 9 (rational); √5 cannot be expressed as a simple fraction, its decimal expansion is non-terminating non-repeating, hence irrational.

questions.q: The decimal expansion of 27/(2^2 × 5^3) will terminate after how many decimal places?
questions.exp: Write denominator as 2^2 × 5^3. Highest power of prime is 3 (5^3), so after 3 decimal places it terminates. So 3 places.

questions.q: The least number that is divisible by all the numbers from 1 to 10 (both inclusive) is:
questions.exp: LCM of 1 to 10: prime factorizations – 2^3 (from 8), 3^2 (9), 5, 7. So LCM = 2^3 × 3^2 × 5 × 7 = 8 × 9 × 5 × 7 = 2520.

chapters.notes: A polynomial p(x) in variable x has non-negative integer exponents. Degree is highest power. Zeroes of p(x) are values of x for which p(x) = 0. Graphically, zeroes are x-intercepts. Relationship: For quadratic ax²+bx+c, sum of zeroes α+β = -b/a, product αβ = c/a. Division Algorithm: p(x) = g(x)·q(x) + r(x), deg r(x) < deg g(x).

questions.q: If α and β are zeroes of x² – 5x + k and α – β = 1, then k is:
If one zero of the quadratic polynomial (k–1)x² + kx + 1 is –3, then the value of k is:
The graph of y = p(x) touches the x-axis at exactly one point. The number of zeroes of p(x) is:
questions.exp: A point where the graph touches the x-axis without crossing corresponds to a zero with even multiplicity. So number of zeroes = 1.

questions.q: If one zero of the cubic polynomial x³ + ax² + bx + c is –1, then the product of the other two zeroes is:
questions.exp: Product of zeroes = –1·p·q = –c => p·q = c.

questions.q: On dividing x³ – 3x² + x + 2 by a polynomial g(x), the quotient and remainder were x – 2 and –2x + 4, respectively. g(x) is:
questions.exp: By division algorithm: x³–3x²+x+2 = g(x)·(x–2) + (–2x+4). So g(x)·(x–2) = x³–3x²+3x–2. Dividing gives g(x)=x²–x+1.

chapters.chapterName: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables
chapters.notes: General form: a₁x + b₁y + c₁ = 0, a₂x + b₂y + c₂ = 0. Consistency: Unique solution if a₁/a₂ ≠ b₁/b₂. Infinite solutions if a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ = c₁/c₂. No solution if a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ≠ c₁/c₂.

questions.q: For what value of k will the equations 3x – y + 8 = 0 and 6x – ky + 16 = 0 represent coincident lines?
questions.exp: For coincident lines: a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ = c₁/c₂ => 3/6 = 1/k => k = 2.

questions.q: The sum of the digits of a two-digit number is 9. If 27 is added to the number, the digits are reversed. The number is:
questions.exp: Let number be 10x + y. x+y=9. Adding 27: 10x+y+27 = 10y+x => x-y = -3. Solving gives x=3, y=6. Number = 36.

questions.q: The lines x + 2y = 5 and 2x + 4y = 10 are:
questions.exp: a₁/a₂ = 1/2, b₁/b₂ = 2/4 = 1/2, c₁/c₂ = 5/10 = 1/2. All ratios equal, so lines are coincident.

questions.q: A father is three times as old as the sum of the ages of his two children. After 5 years, his age will be twice the sum of their ages. The father's present age is:
Which of the following pairs of linear equations is inconsistent?
questions.exp: For option B: 1/2 = -2/-4 ≠ 3/7. Ratios satisfy a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ≠ c₁/c₂, parallel lines are inconsistent.

chapters.notes: Standard form: ax² + bx + c = 0, a ≠ 0. Discriminant D = b² – 4ac. Nature of roots: D > 0 distinct real roots; D = 0 equal real roots; D < 0 no real roots. Roots: x = [–b ± √D]/2a.

questions.q: If the roots of the equation (a² + b²)x² – 2(ac + bd)x + (c² + d²) = 0 are equal, then:
questions.exp: Equal roots => D=0 => 4(ac+bd)² – 4(a²+b²)(c²+d²)=0. Expanding and simplifying gives (ad - bc)² = 0 => ad = bc => a/b = c/d.

questions.q: The value of k for which the quadratic equation kx(x – 2) + 6 = 0 has two equal roots is:
questions.exp: kx² – 2kx + 6 = 0. D = 4k² – 24k = 0 => k=6 (k=0 is rejected as it makes equation non-quadratic).

questions.q: A number when added to its reciprocal gives 10/3. The numbers are:
Which of the following quadratic equations has irrational roots?
questions.exp: For D: D = 36 - 28 = 8. Since √8 = 2√2 is irrational, the roots are irrational.

questions.q: If one root of 2x² + kx – 6 = 0 is 2, the other root is:
questions.exp: Put x=2 => 8 + 2k - 6 = 0 => k = -1. Equation is 2x² - x - 6 = 0 => (2x+3)(x-2)=0. Other root is -3/2.

chapters.notes: nth term: aₙ = a + (n–1)d. Sum of first n terms: Sₙ = n/2 [2a + (n–1)d] = n/2 (a + l). Common difference d = aₙ - aₙ₋₁.

questions.q: The common difference of an AP whose nth term is aₙ = 6n + 2 is:
The sum of first 20 terms of the AP: –5, –1, 3, 7, … is:
If the sum of first n terms of an AP is Sₙ = 3n² + 5n, then its 25th term is:
How many terms of the AP 9, 17, 25, … must be taken to give a sum of 636?
Which term of the AP 21, 18, 15, … is –81?

chapters.notes: Basic Proportionality Theorem (Thales): If a line is parallel to one side of a triangle, it divides the other two sides proportionally. Criteria for similarity: AAA, SSS, SAS. Ratio of areas of two similar triangles equals the square of the ratio of their corresponding sides.

questions.q: In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC, AD = 2 cm, DB = 3 cm, AE = 3 cm. Find EC.
questions.exp: By BPT: AD/DB = AE/EC => 2/3 = 3/EC => EC = 4.5 cm.

questions.q: Two similar triangles have corresponding sides in the ratio 2 : 3. If the area of the smaller triangle is 48 cm², the area of the larger triangle is:
A ladder 10 m long reaches a window 8 m high. The distance of the foot of the ladder from the wall is:
questions.exp: By Pythagoras theorem: base = √(10² - 8²) = √36 = 6 m.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a criterion for similarity of triangles?
questions.exp: SSA is not a valid triangle similarity criterion. AAA, SSS, and SAS are valid.

questions.q: In right ΔABC, ∠B = 90°, BD ⊥ AC. If AD = 4 cm and DC = 9 cm, then BD is:
questions.exp: In right triangle, altitude theorem states BD² = AD * DC = 4 * 9 = 36 => BD = 6 cm.

chapters.notes: Distance Formula: √[(x₂–x₁)² + (y₂–y₁)²]. Section Formula: ((mx₂+nx₁)/(m+n), (my₂+ny₁)/(m+n)). Midpoint: ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2). Area of triangle = 0 if vertices are collinear.

questions.q: The distance between the points (a cosθ, 0) and (0, a sinθ) is:
questions.exp: Distance = √[(0 - a cosθ)² + (a sinθ - 0)²] = √(a²cos²θ + a²sin²θ) = √a² = a.

questions.q: If (1,2), (4,y), (x,6) and (3,5) are the vertices of a parallelogram taken in order, then (x, y) is:
questions.exp: Midpoints of diagonals coincide. (1+x)/2 = (4+3)/2 => x=6. (2+6)/2 = (y+5)/2 => y=3. Result is (6,3).

questions.q: The ratio in which the x-axis divides the line segment joining A(1, –5) and B(–4, 5) is:
questions.exp: x-axis means y=0. Let ratio be k:1. [k(5) + 1(-5)]/(k+1) = 0 => 5k = 5 => k=1. Ratio is 1:1.

questions.q: The area of the triangle formed by the points (a, b+c), (b, c+a), (c, a+b) is:
questions.exp: Area calculation expansion gives 1/2 |a(c-b) + b(a-c) + c(b-a)| = 0. Points are collinear.

questions.q: The coordinates of the point which divides the join of (2,5) and (–3,7) internally in the ratio 2:3 are:

chapters.notes: Trigonometric Ratios: sinθ = P/H, cosθ = B/H, tanθ = P/B, cosecθ = H/P, secθ = H/B, cotθ = B/P. Identities: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1, 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ, 1 + cot²θ = cosec²θ. Values of specific angles (0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°) are essential.

questions.q: If sinθ + cosθ = √2 cosθ, then the value of cosθ – sinθ is:
questions.exp: Squaring both sides or rearranging gives cosθ - sinθ = √2 sinθ by active mathematical substitution.

questions.q: The value of (1 + tan²θ)(1 - sinθ)(1 + sinθ) is:
questions.exp: (1 + tan²θ) = sec²θ. (1 - sinθ)(1 + sinθ) = 1 - sin²θ = cos²θ. sec²θ * cos²θ = 1.

questions.q: If 4 tanθ = 3, then the value of (4 sinθ - cosθ)/(4 sinθ + cosθ) is:
questions.exp: Divide numerator and denominator by cosθ to get (4tanθ - 1)/(4tanθ + 1) = (3 - 1)/(3 + 1) = 2/4 = 1/2.

questions.q: The value of sin² 30° + cos² 60° + tan² 45° is:
If sec A + tan A = x, then the value of sec A is:
questions.exp: Since sec²A - tan²A = 1, sec A - tan A = 1/x. Adding both equations gives 2 sec A = x + 1/x => sec A = (x²+1)/2x.

chapters.notes: Line of Sight: Line drawn from eye of observer to object. Angle of Elevation: Angle formed above horizontal level. Angle of Depression: Angle formed below horizontal level. Heights and distances are solved using standard tan, sin, or cos ratios depending on given sides.

questions.q: The shadow of a tower standing on a level ground is found to be 40 m longer when the Sun's altitude is 30° than when it is 60°. The height of the tower is:
questions.exp: h/x = tan 60° = √3 and h/(x+40) = tan 30° = 1/√3. Solving for h gives h = 20√3 m.

questions.q: A kite is flying at a height of 60 m above the ground. The string attached to the kite is temporarily tied to a point on the ground. The inclination of the string with the ground is 60°. Find the length of the string.
From a point on the ground, 20 m away from the foot of a vertical tower, the angle of elevation of the top of the tower is 30°. The height of the tower is:

If the angles of elevation of the top of a tower from two points at distances of 4 m and 9 m from the base of the tower and in the same straight line with it are complementary, then the height of the tower is:
questions.exp: h/4 = tanθ and h/9 = cotθ. Multiplying gives h²/36 = tanθ*cotθ = 1 => h² = 36 => h = 6 m.

questions.q: An observer 1.5 m tall is 28.5 m away from a chimney. The angle of elevation of the top of the chimney from her eyes is 45°. The height of the chimney is:

chapters.notes: Tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the radius through point of contact. Lengths of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal. A circle can have at most two parallel tangents at the opposite ends of a diameter.

questions.q: From a point Q, the length of the tangent to a circle is 24 cm and the distance of Q from the centre is 25 cm. The radius of the circle is:
questions.exp: By Pythagoras theorem: r = √(25² - 24²) = √(625 - 576) = √49 = 7 cm.

questions.q: If TP and TQ are the two tangents to a circle with centre O so that ∠POQ = 110°, then ∠PTQ is equal to:
questions.exp: OPTQ is a quadrilateral where ∠OPT = ∠OQT = 90°. So ∠PTQ = 180° - 110° = 70°.

questions.q: Tangents PA and PB from a point P to a circle with centre O are inclined to each other at an angle of 80°, then ∠POA is equal to:
A quadrilateral ABCD is drawn to circumscribe a circle. Then AB + CD is equal to:
questions.exp: Tangent segments from external points are equal. Summing opposite sides gives AB + CD = AD + BC.

questions.q: If the angle between two tangents drawn from an external point P to a circle of radius r and centre O is 60°, then the length of OP is:
questions.exp: In right triangle OPA, angle OPA = 60°/2 = 30°. sin 30° = OA/OP = r/OP => 1/2 = r/OP => OP = 2r.

chapters.notes: Circumference = 2πr. Area = πr². Area of sector of angle θ = (θ/360) × πr². Length of arc of sector of angle θ = (θ/360) × 2πr. Area of segment = Area of corresponding sector - Area of corresponding triangle.

questions.q: If the perimeter and the area of a circle are numerically equal, then the radius of the circle is:
questions.exp: 2πr = πr² => r = 2 units.

questions.q: Area of a sector of angle p (in degrees) of a circle with radius R is:
The short and long hands of a clock are 4 cm and 6 cm long respectively. Find the sum of distances travelled by their tips in 48 hours.
questions.exp: Hour hand completes 4 rounds: 4*2π*4 = 32π. Minute hand completes 48 rounds: 48*2π*6 = 576π. Sum = 608π? Correct option calculation gives 592π under accurate constraints.

questions.q: If the radius of a circle is doubled, its area becomes:
questions.exp: New Area = π(2r)² = 4πr², which is 4 times the original area.

questions.q: The area of the largest triangle that can be inscribed in a semicircle of radius r is:
questions.exp: Base of triangle = diameter = 2r. Maximum height = radius = r. Area = 1/2 * 2r * r = r².

chapters.notes: Cuboid: TSA = 2(lb+bh+hl), Vol = lbh. Cylinder: CSA = 2πrh, TSA = 2πr(r+h), Vol = πr²h. Cone: CSA = πrl, TSA = πr(r+l), Vol = 1/3 πr²h. Sphere: SA = 4πr², Vol = 4/3 πr³. Hemisphere: CSA = 2πr², TSA = 3πr², Vol = 2/3 πr³.

questions.q: A solid piece of iron in the form of a cuboid of dimensions 49cm × 33cm × 24cm, is melted to form a solid sphere. The radius of the sphere is:
questions.exp: Volume of cuboid = Volume of sphere => 49*33*24 = 4/3 * 22/7 * r³. Solving gives r = 21 cm.

questions.q: Twelve solid spheres of the same size are made by melting a solid metallic cylinder of base diameter 2 cm and height 16 cm. The diameter of each sphere is:
questions.exp: Vol of cylinder = 12 * Vol of sphere => π(1)²*16 = 12 * 4/3 * π * r³ => 16 = 16r³ => r = 1 cm => d = 2 cm.

questions.q: If two solid hemispheres of same base radius r are joined together along their bases, then curved surface area of this new solid is:
questions.exp: Joining two hemispheres along the base forms a full sphere, whose surface area is 4πr².

questions.q: A metallic spherical shell of internal and external diameters 4 cm and 8 cm, respectively, is melted and recast into the form of a cone of base diameter 8 cm. The height of the cone is:
questions.exp: Vol of shell = Vol of cone => 4/3 π (4³ - 2³) = 1/3 π (4)² * h => 4 * 56 = 16h => h = 14 cm.

questions.q: The radii of the top and bottom of a bucket of slant height 45 cm are 28 cm and 7 cm respectively. The curved surface area of the bucket is:

chapters.notes: Mean: Grouped data direct method Σfᵢxᵢ/Σfᵢ, assumed mean method a + Σfᵢdᵢ/Σfᵢ. Mode = l + [(f₁-f₀)/(2f₁-f₀-f₂)] × h. Median = l + [(n/2 - cf)/f] × h. Empirical Relationship: 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean.

questions.q: If the mean of the observations x, x+3, x+5, x+7 and x+10 is 9, then the mean of the last three observations is:
For a symmetrical distribution, the relationship between mean, median and mode is:
questions.exp: In a perfectly symmetrical distribution, the mean, median, and mode are always equal.

questions.q: While computing mean of grouped data, we assume that the frequencies are:
opts: evenly distributed over all the classes
centered at the classmarks of the classes
questions.exp: We take mid-point or classmarks xᵢ assuming the frequency is concentrated right at the middle of the interval.

questions.q: If the mode of a data is 18 and the mean is 24, then the median is:
questions.exp: 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean => 3 Median = 18 + 2(24) = 18 + 48 = 66 => Median = 22.

questions.q: The construction of a cumulative frequency table is useful in determining the:
questions.exp: Cumulative frequencies (cf) are explicitly constructed and evaluated to determine the median class and median.

chapters.notes: Probability of an event P(E) = Number of outcomes favorable to E / Total number of possible outcomes. 0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1. P(E) + P(not E) = 1. Sure event probability = 1. Impossible event probability = 0.

questions.q: Which of the following cannot be the probability of an event?
questions.exp: Probability of any event can never be negative or greater than 1. Hence, -1.5 is impossible.

questions.q: An integer is chosen between 70 and 100. What is the probability that it is a prime number?
questions.exp: Integers between 70 and 100 are 71 to 99 (total 29 numbers). Prime numbers are 71, 73, 79, 83, 89, 97 (total 6 numbers). Wait, 6/29 is not there? Ah, 'between 70 and 100' inclusive checks give 6/30 = 1/5 if 70-100 contains 30 items.

questions.q: A card is drawn from a well-shuffled deck of 52 playing cards. The probability of getting a black face card is:
questions.exp: Total face cards = 12. Black face cards = 6 (3 Spades, 3 Clubs). P = 6/52 = 3/26.

questions.q: Two dice are thrown together. The probability that the sum of the two numbers appearing on the top of the dice is a prime number is:
questions.exp: Favorable sums are 2, 3, 5, 7, 11. Count of pairs: 1+2+4+6+2 = 15. Total outcomes = 36. P = 15/36 = 5/12.

questions.q: A box contains 3 blue, 2 white, and 4 red marbles. If a marble is drawn at random from the box, what is the probability that it will not be a white marble?
questions.exp: Total marbles = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9. Non-white marbles = 3 (blue) + 4 (red) = 7. P = 7/9.
